name: Right atrium
blurb: Upper right chamber that receives deoxygenated systemic blood from the venae cavae and coronary sinus, then fills the right ventricle.
pearl: Houses the SA node in its upper wall near the SVC opening — the heart's natural pacemaker that sets sinus rhythm.

name: Right ventricle
blurb: Anterior-most chamber that pumps deoxygenated blood through the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary trunk toward the lungs.
pearl: Thin-walled, low-pressure pump; affected in inferior MI (RCA) and acutely strained in massive PE (cor pulmonale).

name: Left atrium
blurb: Most posterior chamber; receives oxygenated blood from the four pulmonary veins and delivers it to the left ventricle.
pearl: Enlarges in mitral stenosis (can cause dysphagia/AFib); its appendage is the top site of thrombus in atrial fibrillation.

name: Left ventricle
blurb: Thick-walled chamber forming the apex; ejects oxygenated blood through the aortic valve into the aorta for systemic circulation.
pearl: Thickest myocardium (highest pressure); hypertrophies in chronic HTN/aortic stenosis and drives most heart failure.

name: Aorta (aortic arch)
blurb: Largest artery; arches over the heart carrying oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to the entire systemic circulation.
pearl: Arch branches L→R: brachiocephalic, left common carotid, left subclavian. Common dissection site (tearing chest-to-back pain).

name: Pulmonary trunk
blurb: Large vessel arising from the right ventricle that carries deoxygenated blood and bifurcates into the right and left pulmonary arteries.
pearl: Carries deoxygenated blood (like the umbilical arteries); tethered to the aortic arch by the ligamentum arteriosum (former ductus arteriosus).

name: Superior vena cava
blurb: Large vein draining deoxygenated blood from the head, neck, and upper limbs into the right atrium.
pearl: SVC syndrome (often lung cancer/mediastinal mass) causes facial swelling, plethora, and distended neck veins.

name: Inferior vena cava
blurb: Largest vein; returns deoxygenated blood from the abdomen, pelvis, and lower limbs to the inferior right atrium.
pearl: Pierces the diaphragm at T8, the highest of its three openings — "I 8 (ate) 10 eggs at 12": IVC T8, esophagus T10, aorta T12.

name: Pulmonary arteries
blurb: Right and left branches of the pulmonary trunk that carry deoxygenated blood from the right heart to each lung.
pearl: Main site of pulmonary embolism; a large saddle embolus lodging at the bifurcation can cause sudden death.

name: Pulmonary veins
blurb: Four veins (two per lung) returning oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium.
pearl: Only veins carrying oxygenated blood; ectopic foci near their ostia trigger AFib (targeted in PV isolation ablation).

name: Left anterior descending artery
blurb: Branch of the left coronary artery running in the anterior interventricular groove, supplying the anterior LV and septum.
pearl: The "widow-maker" — supplies the most myocardium; occlusion causes large anterior MI (ST elevation in V1-V4).

name: Right coronary artery
blurb: Runs in the right atrioventricular groove, supplying the right heart, inferior LV, and (usually) the SA and AV nodes.
pearl: Supplies SA/AV nodes in most people; inferior MI (leads II, III, aVF) often causes bradycardia/heart block.

name: Apex of the heart
blurb: Inferolateral tip formed by the left ventricle, pointing down-left toward the 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line.
pearl: Site of the point of maximal impulse (PMI); displaced laterally in LV hypertrophy/dilated cardiomyopathy (cardiomegaly).

name: Frontal lobe
blurb: Anterior-most cerebral lobe (front of central sulcus) governing motor control, executive function, planning, and personality.
pearl: Houses Broca's area (dominant hemisphere) for speech production; damage = expressive (Broca) aphasia with intact comprehension.

name: Parietal lobe
blurb: Cerebral lobe behind central sulcus integrating somatosensory input, spatial awareness, and navigation.
pearl: Non-dominant lesion causes contralateral hemineglect; dominant lesion = Gerstmann syndrome (agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, L/R confusion).

name: Temporal lobe
blurb: Inferolateral lobe below the lateral sulcus handling hearing, memory encoding, and language comprehension.
pearl: Wernicke's area (dominant) lesion = receptive aphasia (fluent but nonsensical speech); contains hippocampus, key to new memory formation.

name: Occipital lobe
blurb: Posterior-most cerebral lobe containing the primary visual cortex for processing visual information.
pearl: Primary visual cortex lies along the calcarine sulcus; PCA stroke causes contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing.

name: Cerebellum
blurb: Posterior fossa structure behind the brainstem coordinating balance, posture, and smooth, accurate movement.
pearl: Lesions cause IPSILATERAL deficits: ataxia, dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia; vermis damage = truncal/gait ataxia.

name: Pons
blurb: Middle brainstem segment bridging cerebrum and cerebellum; relays signals and houses pontine respiratory centers and REM-sleep nuclei.
pearl: Origin of CN V-VIII; basilar pons infarct (or central pontine myelinolysis from rapid Na+ correction) can cause locked-in syndrome.

name: Medulla oblongata
blurb: Lowest brainstem segment continuous with spinal cord, controlling cardiac, respiratory, and vasomotor reflexes.
pearl: Site of pyramidal (motor) decussation; houses vital cardiorespiratory centers, so injury is often fatal. Origin of CN IX-XII.

name: Precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex)
blurb: Gyrus immediately anterior to the central sulcus; the primary motor cortex (M1) initiating voluntary movement.
pearl: Controls CONTRALATERAL body via corticospinal tract; somatotopically mapped (motor homunculus), with face/hand having large representation.

name: Postcentral gyrus (primary somatosensory cortex)
blurb: Gyrus immediately posterior to the central sulcus; primary somatosensory cortex (S1) receiving touch, pain, and proprioception.
pearl: Receives CONTRALATERAL sensation via thalamus (VPL/VPM); somatotopically organized as the sensory homunculus, lips/hands magnified.

name: Central sulcus
blurb: Deep groove (sulcus of Rolando) separating the frontal lobe from the parietal lobe.
pearl: Landmark dividing motor (precentral) from sensory (postcentral) cortex; boundary between frontal and parietal lobes.

name: Lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure)
blurb: Prominent deep cleft separating the temporal lobe below from the frontal and parietal lobes above.
pearl: Overlies the insula; the MCA runs within it, making it a key vascular and surgical landmark on the lateral surface.

name: Corpus callosum
blurb: Large arched band of white matter (commissural fibers) connecting the left and right cerebral hemispheres.
pearl: Largest commissure in the brain; surgical section (callosotomy) for intractable epilepsy can produce split-brain syndrome.

name: Thalamus
blurb: Paired egg-shaped gray-matter mass forming most of the diencephalon; relays sensory and motor signals to the cortex.
pearl: Relays ALL ascending sensory input except smell; lesions can cause Dejerine-Roussy (thalamic pain) syndrome.

name: Hypothalamus
blurb: Small region below the thalamus controlling homeostasis, autonomic function, and the endocrine system via the pituitary.
pearl: Master regulator of temperature, hunger, thirst, and circadian rhythm; links the nervous and endocrine systems via the pituitary.

name: Pituitary gland
blurb: Pea-sized endocrine gland in the sella turcica below the hypothalamus; the body's 'master gland.'
pearl: Adenoma compressing the optic chiasm causes bitemporal hemianopia; anterior lobe makes hormones, posterior stores ADH/oxytocin.

name: Midbrain
blurb: Uppermost brainstem segment connecting pons to diencephalon; relays motor/sensory traffic and controls eye movement and reflexes.
pearl: Houses CN III & IV nuclei and the substantia nigra (dopamine); nigral degeneration underlies Parkinson disease.

name: Pons
blurb: Bulging middle brainstem region linking medulla to midbrain and cerebrum to cerebellum; aids relay and breathing control.
pearl: Contains pontine respiratory (pneumotaxic) centers and CN V-VIII nuclei; central pontine myelinolysis follows rapid hyponatremia correction.

name: Medulla oblongata
blurb: Lowest brainstem segment continuous with the spinal cord; houses vital autonomic centers.
pearl: Controls heart rate, breathing, and blood pressure; site of the pyramidal (motor) decussation giving contralateral control.

name: Cerebellum
blurb: Folded structure dorsal to the brainstem that coordinates movement, balance, posture, and motor learning.
pearl: Coordinates movement ipsilaterally; lesions cause ataxia, intention tremor, dysmetria, and dysdiadochokinesia.

name: Fornix
blurb: Arching C-shaped white-matter tract carrying signals from the hippocampus to the mammillary bodies and hypothalamus.
pearl: Major efferent of the hippocampus and part of the limbic (Papez) circuit; damage impairs forming new memories.

name: Pineal gland
blurb: Small endocrine gland on the epithalamus that secretes melatonin to regulate circadian rhythm.
pearl: Often calcifies (radiographic midline marker); a pineal tumor can cause Parinaud syndrome (upward gaze palsy) and hydrocephalus.

name: Cingulate gyrus
blurb: Arching cortical gyrus just above the corpus callosum; a core component of the limbic system.
pearl: Involved in emotion, motivation, and pain processing; a key node of the limbic Papez circuit.

name: Corpora quadrigemina (tectum)
blurb: Four bumps on the dorsal midbrain (paired superior and inferior colliculi) handling visual and auditory reflexes.
pearl: Superior colliculi = visual/eye-movement reflexes; inferior colliculi = auditory relay; dorsal lesion causes Parinaud syndrome.

name: Larynx
blurb: Cartilaginous 'voice box' between pharynx and trachea (C3-C6) that produces sound and guards the airway during swallowing.
pearl: Intrinsic muscles are all CN X (recurrent laryngeal) except cricothyroid (external br. of superior laryngeal); RLN injury = hoarseness.

name: Trachea
blurb: Flexible windpipe (~10-12 cm) from larynx to the carina, held open by C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings, conducting air to the bronchi.
pearl: Bifurcates at the carina (~T4/T5, sternal angle); posterior trachealis muscle bridges the C-ring gap. Lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium.

name: Right main bronchus
blurb: First airway branch to the right lung; wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left, arising at the carina.
pearl: More vertical/wider = aspirated objects and mis-placed ET tubes preferentially lodge here. Gives 3 lobar bronchi (vs 2 on left).

name: Left main bronchus
blurb: First airway branch to the left lung; narrower, longer, and more horizontal as it passes under the aortic arch.
pearl: Passes beneath the aortic arch; an enlarged left atrium can splay the carina (widened carina on CXR). Branches into only 2 lobar bronchi.

name: Right superior lobe
blurb: Uppermost lobe of the three-lobed right lung, bounded below by the horizontal fissure (and oblique fissure).
pearl: Classic site for reactivation TB (apical/posterior segments) and Pancoast tumors compressing the brachial plexus/sympathetic chain (Horner syndrome).

name: Right middle lobe
blurb: Small wedge-shaped lobe unique to the right lung, lying between the horizontal and oblique fissures.
pearl: The only middle lobe (no left counterpart). 'Right middle lobe syndrome' = recurrent atelectasis/infection from its long, narrow, poorly-draining bronchus.

name: Right inferior lobe
blurb: Largest, most posteroinferior lobe of the right lung, below the oblique fissure, sitting on the diaphragm.
pearl: Aspiration while supine favors the superior segment of the lower lobes; gravity makes the bases the zone of greatest ventilation and perfusion.

name: Left superior lobe
blurb: Upper lobe of the two-lobed left lung, bearing the lingula and the cardiac notch that accommodates the heart.
pearl: The lingula is the left-lung homolog of the right middle lobe; the cardiac notch and thin lingular margin make room for the left-shifted heart.

name: Left inferior lobe
blurb: Lower lobe of the left lung, separated from the superior lobe by the single oblique fissure, resting on the diaphragm.
pearl: Left lung has only one (oblique) fissure and 2 lobes because cardiac space displaces the middle lobe; lower-lobe bases collapse first when bedridden.

name: Bronchioles
blurb: Small (<1 mm) cartilage-free airways distal to bronchi, walled by smooth muscle, that regulate airflow and end in respiratory bronchioles/alveoli.
pearl: Lack cartilage so they constrict in asthma (beta-2 agonists dilate); terminal bronchiole = end of conducting zone, respiratory bronchiole = start of gas exchange.

name: Diaphragm
blurb: Dome-shaped skeletal muscle separating thorax from abdomen; the primary muscle of inspiration, flattening to draw air in.
pearl: Innervated by the phrenic nerve (C3-C5, 'C3-4-5 keeps the diaphragm alive'); openings: IVC T8, esophagus T10, aorta T12.

name: Renal cortex
blurb: Outer reddish-brown layer of the kidney beneath the capsule; contains glomeruli and convoluted tubules for blood filtration.
pearl: Houses all glomeruli (renal corpuscles) and PCT/DCT; high cortical perfusion per gram makes it the prime site of ATN from ischemia/toxins.

name: Renal medulla
blurb: Inner kidney region organized into pyramids; contains loops of Henle and collecting ducts that concentrate urine.
pearl: Steep medullary osmotic gradient (countercurrent multiplier) concentrates urine; relatively hypoxic, so prone to ischemic injury and papillary necrosis.

name: Renal pyramid
blurb: Cone-shaped striated subunits of the medulla whose apex (papilla) drains urine into a minor calyx.
pearl: Each kidney has ~8-18 pyramids; one pyramid plus overlying cortex = a renal lobe; papillae fail in papillary necrosis (diabetes, NSAIDs, sickle cell).

name: Renal column
blurb: Cortical tissue (columns of Bertin) extending inward between adjacent renal pyramids, carrying interlobar vessels.
pearl: Interlobar arteries/veins run within renal columns; a hypertrophied column of Bertin can mimic a renal mass on imaging.

name: Renal pelvis
blurb: Funnel-shaped collecting space formed by the major calyces that funnels urine into the ureter at the hilum.
pearl: Distention from outflow obstruction = hydronephrosis; urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma can arise here, classically with painless hematuria.

name: Major calyx
blurb: Two to three large urine-collecting chambers formed by merging minor calyces, draining into the renal pelvis.
pearl: Blunting/clubbing of calyces on imaging suggests chronic pyelonephritis or reflux nephropathy; usually 2-3 major calyces per kidney.

name: Minor calyx
blurb: Small cup-shaped chambers that surround each renal papilla and collect urine, then merge into major calyces.
pearl: First to receive urine from the papilla; ~7-9 per kidney, and calyceal blunting indicates papillary/parenchymal damage.

name: Ureter
blurb: Muscular tube carrying urine from the renal pelvis to the bladder via peristalsis of its smooth-muscle wall.
pearl: Three constrictions (UPJ, pelvic brim/iliac vessels, ureterovesical junction) are the classic sites where kidney stones impact; UVJ is narrowest.

name: Renal artery
blurb: Paired artery branching from the abdominal aorta (~L1-L2) supplying oxygenated blood to the kidney.
pearl: Renal artery stenosis causes secondary HTN via renin release; fibromuscular dysplasia (young women, 'string of beads') vs atherosclerosis (older).

name: Renal vein
blurb: Paired vein draining the kidney into the inferior vena cava; the longer left vein crosses anterior to the aorta.
pearl: Left renal vein takes the left gonadal/suprarenal veins and is compressed in nutcracker syndrome (between SMA and aorta); left varicocele clue.

name: Renal capsule
blurb: Tough fibrous outer covering enclosing the kidney parenchyma and protecting it from infection and trauma.
pearl: Non-distensible capsule means swelling (pyelonephritis, obstruction) stretches it, causing flank pain; subcapsular hematoma can cause Page kidney HTN.

name: Renal hilum
blurb: Medial concave indentation where vessels, nerves, lymphatics, and the ureter enter and exit the kidney.
pearl: Anterior-to-posterior order = renal Vein, Artery, then ureter/Pelvis; the vein is most anterior and the ureter most posterior.

name: Dendrites
blurb: Branched tapering processes extending from the soma that receive incoming signals and conduct graded potentials toward the cell body.
pearl: Receptive zone of the neuron; dendritic spines are sites of synaptic plasticity (LTP) and are reduced/abnormal in fragile X and intellectual disability.

name: Soma (cell body)
blurb: Metabolic center of the neuron housing the nucleus and most organelles; integrates inputs and maintains the cell.
pearl: Contains Nissl bodies (rough ER); chromatolysis (Nissl dispersal) signals axonal injury and follows axotomy.

name: Nucleus
blurb: Membrane-bound organelle in the soma containing the neuron's DNA and a prominent nucleolus; directs gene expression and protein synthesis.
pearl: Large, pale, with a prominent nucleolus ('owl-eye'); neurons are post-mitotic (G0), so most cannot replace themselves if lost.

name: Axon hillock
blurb: Cone-shaped region of the soma where the axon originates; summed graded potentials converge here before the initial segment.
pearl: The adjacent initial segment is the trigger zone: highest Na+ channel density and lowest threshold, so the action potential is initiated there.

name: Axon
blurb: Single long process conducting action potentials away from the soma toward the axon terminals.
pearl: Conduction velocity rises with diameter and myelination; lacks rough ER, so it relies on axonal transport (kinesin anterograde, dynein retrograde).

name: Myelin sheath
blurb: Lipid-rich insulating wrapping around the axon, made by Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS), that speeds conduction.
pearl: Enables saltatory conduction; CNS loss in MS, PNS loss in Guillain-Barre; one oligodendrocyte myelinates many axons, one Schwann cell one segment.

name: Node of Ranvier
blurb: Periodic unmyelinated gap in the sheath, densely packed with voltage-gated Na+ channels, where the action potential regenerates.
pearl: Site of saltatory conduction: the impulse 'jumps' node to node; demyelination disrupts nodal channels and slows or blocks conduction.

name: Axon terminals
blurb: Distal branched endings of the axon that form synapses and release neurotransmitter to communicate with target cells.
pearl: Voltage-gated Ca2+ influx triggers SNARE-mediated vesicle fusion; botulinum and tetanus toxins cleave SNAREs to block transmitter release.

name: Schwann cell
blurb: Peripheral glial cell that myelinates or ensheaths PNS axons and forms the neurilemma needed for nerve regeneration.
pearl: Myelinates ONE PNS segment (vs many for oligodendrocytes); its neurilemma guides PNS axon regrowth; transformed in schwannomas (e.g. vestibular schwannoma).

name: Cornea
blurb: Transparent avascular dome at the front of the eye that refracts incoming light and protects the anterior structures.
pearl: Provides ~two-thirds of the eye's refractive power; avascular, gets O2 from tears/aqueous; ideal for transplant (low rejection).

name: Iris
blurb: Pigmented contractile diaphragm between cornea and lens that controls pupil size and thus how much light enters.
pearl: Sphincter pupillae (parasympathetic, CN III) constricts; dilator pupillae (sympathetic) dilates. Stromal melanin sets eye color.

name: Pupil
blurb: Central adjustable aperture in the iris through which light passes to reach the lens and retina.
pearl: Constricts to light (CN II afferent / CN III efferent); fixed dilated pupil suggests CN III compression (e.g. uncal herniation).

name: Lens
blurb: Transparent biconvex body behind the iris that fine-tunes focus by changing shape (accommodation).
pearl: Accommodation: ciliary muscle contracts (CN III) to round the lens for near vision. Opacification = cataract.

name: Retina
blurb: Innermost neural layer lining the posterior eye that transduces light into neural signals via photoreceptors.
pearl: Rods (dim/peripheral), cones (color/acuity); light hyperpolarizes photoreceptors. Detachment is an ophthalmologic emergency.

name: Optic nerve
blurb: Cranial nerve II carrying visual signals from the retinal ganglion cells to the brain, exiting at the optic disc.
pearl: Optic disc = blind spot (no photoreceptors); CN II is a CNS tract myelinated by oligodendrocytes, hence optic neuritis in MS.

name: Sclera
blurb: Tough opaque white outer fibrous coat that maintains eye shape and serves as attachment for extraocular muscles.
pearl: Continuous anteriorly with the cornea at the limbus; yellowing (icterus) signals hyperbilirubinemia/jaundice.

name: Choroid
blurb: Highly vascular pigmented middle layer between sclera and retina that nourishes the outer retina and absorbs stray light.
pearl: Part of the uveal tract (with iris + ciliary body); supplies outer retinal layers. Inflammation = uveitis.

name: Vitreous body
blurb: Clear gel filling the large posterior cavity behind the lens, maintaining eye shape and supporting the retina.
pearl: Does not regenerate if removed. Posterior vitreous detachment causes floaters/flashes and can lead to retinal tears.

name: Ciliary body
blurb: Ring of muscle and epithelium behind the iris that secretes aqueous humor and controls lens shape via zonular fibers.
pearl: Ciliary epithelium makes aqueous humor; muscle contraction (CN III) enables accommodation. Drug target in glaucoma.

name: Fovea centralis
blurb: Tiny central pit within the macula of the retina that provides the sharpest, most detailed central vision.
pearl: Highest acuity: all cones, near 1:1 cone-to-ganglion ratio, no overlying vessels. Degenerates in age-related macular degeneration.

name: Anterior chamber
blurb: Aqueous-humor-filled space between the cornea and the iris that nourishes the cornea and lens.
pearl: Aqueous drains via trabecular meshwork into the canal of Schlemm; blocked outflow raises IOP, causing glaucoma.

name: Auricle (pinna)
blurb: Visible elastic-cartilage flap of the outer ear that collects and funnels sound waves into the external acoustic meatus.
pearl: Made of elastic cartilage (not bone); the lobule is the only fleshy, cartilage-free part, used for piercings and blood draws.

name: External acoustic meatus
blurb: ~2.5 cm canal from the auricle to the tympanic membrane that conducts sound and houses cerumen-producing glands.
pearl: Lateral third cartilaginous, medial two-thirds bony; pull pinna up/back in adults (down/back in kids) to straighten it for otoscopy.

name: Tympanic membrane
blurb: Thin cone-shaped membrane separating outer from middle ear that vibrates in response to sound and drives the ossicles.
pearl: Cone of light points anteroinferior; sensory innervation by CN V3, IX, X; bulging/erythema seen in acute otitis media.

name: Malleus
blurb: First and largest auditory ossicle; its handle attaches to the tympanic membrane and its head articulates with the incus.
pearl: First ossicle in the chain ("hammer"); tensor tympani (CN V3) inserts on it and dampens loud sounds to protect the ear.

name: Incus
blurb: Middle anvil-shaped ossicle that bridges the malleus and stapes, transmitting vibrations across the middle ear.
pearl: Long process is the most common ossicle to undergo avascular necrosis/erosion in chronic otitis media or cholesteatoma.

name: Stapes
blurb: Smallest bone in the body; its footplate sits in the oval window, transferring vibration into the inner-ear fluid.
pearl: Stapedius (CN VII) dampens it; otosclerosis fixes the footplate causing conductive hearing loss, treated by stapedectomy.

name: Cochlea
blurb: Spiral inner-ear structure containing the organ of Corti, which transduces fluid vibrations into auditory nerve signals.
pearl: Tonotopic: base detects high frequencies, apex low; outer hair cell loss from noise/aminoglycosides causes sensorineural loss.

name: Semicircular canals
blurb: Three orthogonal fluid-filled loops of the inner ear that sense rotational (angular) head acceleration for balance.
pearl: Crista ampullaris/cupula detect angular acceleration; displaced otoconia here cause BPPV (Dix-Hallpike, treated by Epley).

name: Vestibule
blurb: Central inner-ear chamber housing the utricle and saccule, sensing linear acceleration and head position relative to gravity.
pearl: Macula of utricle (horizontal) and saccule (vertical) contain otoliths detecting linear acceleration and static head tilt.

name: Eustachian (auditory) tube
blurb: Canal linking the middle ear to the nasopharynx that equalizes middle-ear pressure with atmospheric pressure.
pearl: Shorter, more horizontal in children, so infections ascend easily, predisposing to recurrent otitis media.

name: Vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII)
blurb: Cranial nerve VIII carrying hearing (cochlear) and balance (vestibular) signals from the inner ear to the brainstem.
pearl: Vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) at the cerebellopontine angle causes unilateral SNHL, tinnitus; linked to NF2.
